coredata.dc:title: Detection of HepG2 Cells in Artificial Samples by Multifunctional Microfluidic Chip
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2017 Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: ZHANG, Ze-Jie
SU, Xi
XU, Yi
CHEN, Li
coredata.dc:description: A multifunctional microfluidic chip integrated with multi-orifice flow fractionation (MOFF) and magnetic capture technique was developed to specifically separate and capture HepG2 cells in artificial samples. The chip contained a glass substrate and a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) micro-channel cover plate. The PDMS cover plate consisted of three 10-mm-long injection channels, a MOFF separation zone and a hexagonal cavity cell enrichment detection zone. The MOFF separation zone, with a total length of 20 mm, was consisted of 80 semi-rhombic shrinkage and expansion units with a length of 0.18 mm, a depth of 50 μm, a shrinkage area width of 0.06 mm, and an expansion area of 0.20 mm. The angle between each group of shrinkage and expansion units was 103.0°. In this work, HepG2-blood cell suspension was used as the sample. Based on the principle that the magnetic beads surface modified c-Met antibody could specifically bind to HepG2 cells, an immune-magnetic beads (anti-MNCs) suspension at a concentration of 50 μg mL−1 was prepared by surface carboxylated beads, EDC (1 mg mL−1), NHS (1 mg mL−1) and c-Met antibody. At the optimized flow rate of 50 μL min−1, HepG2 in suspension samples was efficiently captured at the detection zone of chip via a magnetic field. The carbon quantum dots were prepared by microwave heating with citric acid and thiourea to label HepG2 cells which made it possible that the captured HepG2 could be visualized in-situ. The captured cells in the chip detection area were counted by microscope. It was shown that the capture rate of HepG2 cells was 88.5% ± 6.7% (500 μL volume contained 106 blood cells and 10 HepG2 cells). The results demonstrated the developed multifunctional microfluidic chip may serve as a promising tool for separation and capture of tumour cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microfluidic chip
Multi-orifice flow fractionation
Magnetic capture
Fluorescence visualization
HepG2

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2017 Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: CHINESE JOURNAL OF ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY Volume 45, Issue 11, November 2017 Online English edition of the Chinese language journal Cite this article as: Chin J Anal Chem, 2017, 45(11), 1589â€“1594. ________________________ Received 13 July 2017; accepted 6 September 2017 *Corresponding author. Email: xuyibbd@sina.com This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21375156), the National Migh-Tech R&D Program (863 program. No. 2015AA021104), the Frontier Research Key Projects of Chongqing Science and Technology Committee (No. cstc2015jcyjBX0010), and the Scientific and Technical Innovation Projects for People's Livelihood of Chongqing Science and Technology Committee (No. cstc2015shmszx00014). Copyright Â© 2017, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier Limited. All rights reserved. DOI: 10.1016/S1872-2040(17)61046-6 RESEARCH PAPER Detection of HepG2 Cells in Artificial Samples by Multifunctional Microfluidic Chip ZHANG Ze-Jie2,4, SU Xi1,2,4, XU Yi1,2,3,4,*, CHEN Li1,3,4,* 1 Key Disciplines Lab of Novel Micro-nano Devices and System Technology, Chongqing 400044, China 2 School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400044, China 3 School of Optoelectronic Engineering, Research Center of Microsystem, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400044, China 4 International R & D center of Micro-nano Systems and New Materials Technology, Chongqing 400044, China Abstract: A multifunctional microfluidic chip integrated with multi-orifice flow fractionation (MOFF) and magnetic capture technique was developed to specifically separate and capture HepG2 cells in artificial samples. The chip contained a glass substrate and a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) micro-channel cover plate. The PDMS cover plate consisted of three 10-mm-long injection channels, a MOFF separation zone and a hexagonal cavity cell enrichment detection zone. The MOFF separation zone, with a total length of 20 mm, was consisted of 80 semi-rhombic shrinkage and expansion units with a length of 0.18 mm, a depth of 50 Î¼m, a shrinkage area width of 0.06 mm, and an expansion area of 0.20 mm. The angle between each group of shrinkage and expansion units was 103.0Â°. In this work, HepG2-blood cell suspension was used as the sample. Based on the principle that the magnetic beads surface modified c-Met antibody could specifically bind to HepG2 cells, an immune-magnetic beads (anti-MNCs) suspension at a concentration of 50 Î¼g mLâ€“1 was prepared by surface carboxylated beads, EDC (1 mg mLâ€“1), NHS (1 mg mLâ€“1) and c-Met antibody. At the optimized flow rate of 50 Î¼L minâ€“1, HepG2 in suspension samples was efficiently captured at the detection zone of chip via a magnetic field. The carbon quantum dots were prepared by microwave heating with citric acid and thiourea to label HepG2 cells which made it possible that the captured HepG2 could be visualized in-situ .The captured cells in the chip detection area were counted by microscope. It was shown that the capture rate of HepG2 cells was 88.5% Â± 6.7% (500 Î¼L volume contained 106 blood cells and 10 HepG2 cells). The results demonstrated the developed multifunctional microfluidic chip may serve as a promising tool for separation and capture of tumour cells. Key Words: Microfluidic chip; Multi-orifice flow fractionation; Magnetic capture; Fluorescence visualization; HepG2 1 Introduction Circulating tumour cells (CTCs) detection is significant for cancer patients, mainly in the early diagnosis of cancer, the judgment of tumor metastasis, the clinical treatment of cancer patients and the evaluation of therapeutic effect[1,2]. At present, the microfluidic cell chip has been extensively studied and followed in the field of CTCs because of its high throughput, high content and low sample demand[3]. Physical separation based on the microfluidic chip for cells is simple to operate and can separate target analyte rapidly. It mainly includes centrifugal separation based on cell size, multi-orifice flow fractionation (MOFF) and magnetic separation[4,5]. Based on the difference of cell size, 2â€“4 mL of breast cancer cells-blood cellsâ€™ artificial samples were separated using centrifugation on the chip by Lee et al[6]. The separation of breast cancer cells were achieved within 30 s, and the separation efficiency was 61%. Microfluidic chip based on centrifugal technique has the advantage of rapid separation, but the centrifugal process is easy to loss part of tumor cells, ZHANG Ze-Jie et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2017, 45(11): 1589â€“1594 resulting in lower separation efficiency. According to the fact that the particles with different sizes in the shrinkage/expansion porous channel had different hydrodynamic inertia force effect, Hyun et al[7] used a MOFF to separate 24 groups of 7.5 mL of artificial samples within 30 min, the number of separated tumor cells was 0â€“21. Shen et al[8] used MOFF chip to separate tumor cells from artificial samples at the separation rate of 2.24 Ã— 107 cells per minute. The separation process of single MOFF mode was fast, but the cells were usually detected in the off-chip, and could not achieve on-chip detection. Tang et al[9] achieved MCF-7 cells from 600â€“800 Î¼L blood samples via adsorbed the magnetic beads-cell complex in a parallel array of nickel-nickel arrays with an external magnetic field, and the separation rate was 43.0% Â± 1.2% (n = 3). Kang et al[10] successfully isolated the rat M6C breast cancer cells using the same method. The magnetic separation method has the advantage of in situ detection, but the natural sedimentation and non-specific adsorption of a large number of blood cells in the blood sample will affect the separation effect. Now, there are many studies on the various single cell separation models, but the combination separation models to achieve efficient separation are few. Therefore, it is still a hot topic to integrate multi-mode separation. In recent years, the fluorescence detection has become the common detection technique with its high sensitivity and easy to integrate on microfluidic chip[11,12]. The carbon quantum dots prepared through the typical sucrose-oleic acid heating method by Chen et al[13] and microwave digestion of thiourea- citric acid by Li et al[14] were both successfully used for fluorescence labeling of tumor cells. The research showed that carbon quantum dots were of great interest in cell imaging and detection because of their stable fluorescence performance, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, easy large-scale synthesis functional modification, and low cost of preparation[15â€“17]. In this work, a combination between MOFF separation and magnetic separation were proposed. For HepG2-blood cells suspension sample, most of the blood cells were separated by MOFF, and then the second magnetic separation and capture of the target tumor cells were carried out under the external magnetic field. HepG2 cells were labeled with carbon quantum dots and immune-magnetic beads. At the optimized flow rate of 50 Î¼L minâ€“1, a few HepG2 in suspension sample were efficiently captured in the detection zone of chip via a magnetic field. Finally, the captured cells were counted by microscope. 2 Experimental 2.1 Instruments and reagents Microfluidic syringe pump (70-4505, HARVARD Apparatus), Fluorescent Inverted microscope (OLYMPUS-IX71, Olympus Corporation, Japan), UV-Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan) were used in this study. NaCl, Na2HPO4â€¢12H2O, KH2PO4 (AR grade) were purchased from Chongqing Chuandong Chemical industry company, Ltd (China). Citric acid (AR grade) was from Chongqing Boyi Chemical regent company, Ltd (China). Thiourea (AR grade) was purchased from Chongqing Chemical reagent factory (China). Carboxyl modified Fe3O4 nanoparticles (150 nm) was purchased from Tianjin Beisile Chromatography technology development center (China). c-Met (E999) pAb was from Bioworld Technology, USA). NHS (AR) was purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical reagent factory (China). EDC (98.5%) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical technology company, Ltd (China). Phosphate buffer solution (PBS), HepG2 suspension 104 cell mLâ€“1 and red blood cell suspension 107 cell mLâ€“1 were prepared in this experiment. 2.2 Experimental method 2.2.1 Design and fabrication of microfluidic chip As shown in Fig.1, the microfluidic chip contained a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) micro- channel cover and a glass substrate. The PDMS cover plate consisted of three 10-mm-long injection channels, a MOFF separation zone and a hexagonal cavity cell enrichment detection zone. The MOFF separation zone with a total length of 20 mm, consisted of eighty semi-rhombic shrinkage/expansion units with a length of 0.18 mm, a depth of 50 Î¼m, a shrinkage area width of 0.06 Fig.1 (A) Scheme of structure and size of MOFF; (B) Figure of PDMS cover plate ZHANG Ze-Jie et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2017, 45(11): 1589â€“1594 mm, and an expansion area of 0.20 mm. The angle between each group of shrinkage/expansion units was 103.0Â° (Fig.1A). The microfluidic cover plate involved was prepared by molding (Fig.1B). 2.2.2 Preparation of carbon quantum dots [14] Approximately 0.5 g of citric acid and 0.5 g of thiourea were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water. The mixture was then put into a microwave oven (700 W) for 7 min until the solution changed from colorless to dark brown. After cooling to room temperature, the dark brown solution was centrifuged under 10000 rpm for 5 min, then the particles were filtered through 0.22 Î¼m filter membrane and stored at 4 Â°C in the dark to obtain carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed for the prepared carbon quantum dots to test their fluorescence performance. 2.2.3 Preparation of immune-magnetic beads Approximately 1 mL of NHS (1 mg mLâ€“1) and 1 mL of EDC (1 mg mLâ€“1) were put into 500 Î¼g of surface carboxylated beads. The reaction was carried out under shaking at 150 rpm for 30 min at room temperature to activate the carboxyl groups on the surface of magnetic beads. Then 8 Î¼L of c-Met antibody was added and incubated for 5 h to prepare immune-magnetic beads, which were then collected by external magnetic field and washed three times with PBS. The immune-magnetic beads (anti- MNCs) were incubated with 500 Î¼L of BSA (1 mg mLâ€“1) at room temperature for 3 h, and then re-suspended in PBS to a final concentration of 50 Î¼g mLâ€“1 suspension of anti-MNCs, finally stored at 4 Â°C. 2.2.4 Labelling the HepG2 cells in blood with CQDs and immune-magnetic beads HepG2 cells (104 mLâ€“1) were mixed with blood cells (107 mLâ€“1) at a volume ratio of 1:1 to obtain the mixture cell suspension. Approximately 500 Î¼L of CQDs prepared in Section 2.2.2 was put into 100 Î¼L of the mixture cell suspension above. The suspension was then incubated at 150 rpm for 30 min in a constant temperature shaker, and centrifuged 3 times. After that, 1 mL of anti-MNCs (100 Î¼g mLâ€“1) prepared in Section 2.2.3 were put into the mixture cell suspension, followed by an incubation at 37 Â°C for 150 min under shaking at 150 rpm to obtain HepG2 cells complex labeled by CQDs and immune-magnetic beads. 2.2.5 Visualization of CTCs in artificial samples on microfluidic chip Ten HepG2 cells prepared by dilution at a concentration were added to 500 Î¼L of blood cell suspension. Then 500 Î¼L of CQDs and anti-MNCs were put into blood cell suspension according to the operation method in Section 2.2.4 to prepare the artificial sample of CTCs. As shown in Fig.2, inlets (a, b and c) and outlets (d, e and f) of the microfluidic chip were connected with microfluidic syringe pump. After cleaning the micro-channels between inlets and outlets with anhydrous ethanol and PBS buffer, the mixed labelling cell suspension were introduced from inlet (a) and PBS was introduced from inlets (b and c) at the flow rate of 30, 40, 50, 60 Î¼L minâ€“1 respectively to optimize the flow rate of the fluid in the chip channels. As the experimental steps above, at the optimized flow rate, the number of captured HepG2 cells from 500 Î¼L of artificial sample on microfluidic chip via external magnetic field was observed by fluorescence inverted microscopy in the enrichment area of chip. The experiment was repeated for 20 times. 3 Results and discussion 3.1 CTCs detection strategy on microfluidic chip Analytical process of microfluidic chip is shown in Fig2. First, the initial separation of HepG2 from artificial sample was achieved via MOFF chip. In the micro-channel with a Fig.2 Analytical process of HepG2 cells on microfluidic chip integrated with MOFF separation, magnetic enrichment and fluorescent detection ZHANG Ze-Jie et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2017, 45(11): 1589â€“1594 series of shrinkage/expansion units, the inertial force was induced by the inertial lift and dynamic change of the particles in the fluid, and used for continuous separation of spherical particles of different sizes. A large amount of red blood cells were separated via the shrinkage/expansion unit. The process can not only avoid cell damage, but also avoid unnecessary non-specific deposition to achieve rapid separation. Then, after HepG2 cells were labeled with CQDs and anti-MNCs, the labeled HepG2 cells were subjected to the second step of magnetic enrichment on the chip detection region. It was beneficial to achieve the magnetic enrichment because the chip detection area was larger than channel. When flowing from the narrow MOFF channel to the enrichment area, the fluid flow rate slowed down, which was more conducive to capture HepG2 cells in the region, and easy to observe microscopic fluorescence. 3.2 Labelling the HepG2 cells in blood with CQDs and immune-magnetic beads CQDs, as fluorescent nanoparticles to label the cells, have a great advantage in cell detection and imaging. CQDs adhere well to the cell surface in the cell sample, and nanoparticles can enter cells through cell endocytosis to achieve cell fluorescent labeling. In this study, citric acid and thiourea were used to prepare CQDs with particle size range of 2â€“4 nm[14], and the prepared CQDs were subjected to fluorescence test (Fig.3A). The excitation and emission wavelengths of CQDs were in the range of 420â€“530 nm, and the maximum excitation wavelength was 430 nm. The emission wavelength was 520 nm, followed by a green fluorescence. As shown in Fig.3B, the results showed that the prepared CQDs had good fluorescence performance, and could meet the requirement of fluorescence markers on the microfluidic chip to observe HepG2 cells. 3.3 Effect of flow rate to separation When the sample flow rate was set at 30â€“40 Î¼L minâ€“1, the cancer cells and red blood cells could not be separated. Part of the cancer cells flowed into the outlets (e and f), which caused the loss of tumor cells (Fig.2). Also the cells were easy to settle at this flow rate. When the flow rate was 50 Î¼L minâ€“1, the vast majority of blood cells were discharged from outlets â€œeâ€� and â€œfâ€�, and HepG2 cells and a few blood cells into the enrichment area. HepG2 cells in this region were enriched by external magnetic field, and the extra blood cells were discharged from outlet â€œdâ€�. When the flow rate was 60 Î¼L minâ€“1, the HepG2 cells in the magnetic capture region were washed away. Therefore, the optimum flow rate was 50 Î¼L minâ€“1. Figure 4A illustrates the flow of blood cells and HepG2 cells at the optimal flow rate. It can be seen that a large number of blood cells were separated from the outlets (e and f) and in the region (g), while the larger HepG2 cells enter the magnetic capture zone from the region (h). The above results showed that the designed multi-functional chip could successfully achieve a large number of blood cell separation at the flow rate of 50 Î¼L minâ€“1. 3.4 Visual analysis of HepG2 cells in artificial samples HepG2 cells in artificial samples were labeled with carbon quantum dots and magnetic beads. The 500 Î¼L samples (containing 10 HepG2 cells labeled with carbon quantum dots and immune-magnetic beads) were separated and analyzed by fluorescence microscopy at the optimized flow rate of 50 Î¼L minâ€“1. The captured cells were counted on the enrichment region. The whole process from cells labeling to visualization was only 70 min. According to the formula (1) to calculate the capture efficiency, and the results are showed in Table 1. Capture rate (%) = N1/N Ã— 100% (1) where, N1 is the number of captured HepG2 cells; N2 is the number of HepG2 cells in the sample. Labeled HepG2 cells were observed on the enrichment region by fluorescence inverted microscopy (Fig.4B) at the optimized sample flow rate and external magnetic field. By counting the number of captured cells, the capture efficiency was 88.5% Â± 6.7% (n = 20). The results showed that the designed MOFF multifunctional microfluidic chip integrated with separation, magnetic capture and in situ fluorescence Fig.3 (A) Fluorescence emission spectrum of carbon quantum dots under different excitation wavelength; (B) Fluorescence micrograph of carbon quantum dots labeled HepG2; (C) Image of HepG2 connected immune-magnetic beads at 30 min; (D) Micrograph of HepG2 cells labeled by CQDs and c-met immune-magnetic beads ZHANG Ze-Jie et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2017, 45(11): 1589â€“1594 Table 1 Results of HepG2 cells captured from artificial samples on microfluidic chip Sample volume Blood cells HepG2 cells Recovery Frequency Capture rate (%) Sample 500 Î¼L 5 Ã— 106 10 10 2 88.5 Â± 6.7 9 14 8 3 7 1 Fig.4 (A) Micrographs of â€œg, hâ€� areas at sampling rate of 50 Î¼L minâ€“1; (B) Micrograph of HepG2 on microfluidic chip capture area observation was suitable for the separation, enrichment and visualization of tumor cells in the blood. 4 Conclusions A multifunctional microfluidic chip integrated with separation, magnetic capture and in situ fluorescence observation was designed and fabricated in this study. When the designed chip applied for the samples of blood cells-HepG2 suspension, a large number of blood cells could be isolated in the MOFF region at flow rate of 50 Î¼L minâ€“1, which reduced the interference in the enrichment process of HepG2 cells. HepG2 cells labeled with immune-magnetic beads were subjected to secondary enrichment by external magnetic field. The visualization of HepG2 cells was achieved by CQDs which had the advantage such as simple preparation and good biocompatibility. The capture efficiency of HepG2 cells was quantitatively analyzed by fluorescence inverted microscopy. The capture efficiency of the samples was 88.5% Â± 6.7% (n = 20). This method integrated the separation and enrichment and fluorescence observation of cells. The micro-fluidic chip can be used to analyze the sample of tumor cells-blood cells, and provides the basis for the rapid detection of CTCs. References [1] Cristofanilli M, Hayes D F, Budd G T, Ellis M J, Stopeck A, Reuben J M, Doyle G V, Matera J, Allard W J, Miller M C, Fritsche H A, Hortobagyi G N, Terstappen L W. J. Clin. Oncol., 2005, 23(7): 1420â€“1430 [2] Smerage J B, Hayes D F. Cancer Invest, 2008, 26: 109â€“114 [3] Huo D Q, Guo M Y, Deng Y, Yang M, Fa H B, Hou C J. Chinese J. Anal. Chem., 2015, 43(12): 1801â€’1807 [4] Myung J H, Hong S. Lab Chip, 2015, 15: 4500â€“4511 [5] Hu N, Yang J, Hou W S, Zheng X L, Cao Y, Yang J, Xu R, Zhang R Q. Chem. J. Chinese Universities, 2009, 30(1): 42â€“45 [6] Lee A, Park J, Lim M, Sunkara V, Kim S Y, Kim G H, Kim M H, Cho Y K. Anal. Chem., 2014, 86: 11349âˆ’11356 [7] Hyun K A, Kwon K, Han H, Kim S, Jung H. Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 2013, 40: 206â€“212 [8] Shen S F, Ma C, Zhao L, Wang Y L, Wang J C, Xu J, Li T B, Pang L, Wang J Y. Lab Chip, 2014, 14: 2525â€“2538 [9] Tang M, Wen C Y, Wu L L, Hong S L, Hu J, Xu C M, Pang D W, Zhang Z L. Lab Chip, 2016, 16: 1214â€“1223 [10] Kang J H, Krause S, Tobin H, Mammoto A, Kanapathipillai M, Ingber D E. Lab Chip, 2012, 12: 2175â€“2181 [11] Yu T, Auguste D T. Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 2016, 81: 431â€“437 [12] Galletti G, Sung M S, Vahdat L T, Shah M A, Santana S M, Altavill G, Kirbyd B J, Giannakakou P. Lab Chip, 2014, 14: 147â€“156 [13] Chen B S, Li F M, Li S X, Weng W, Guo H X, Guo T, Zhang X Y, Chen Y B, Huang T T, Hong X L, You S Y, Lin Y M, Zeng K H, Chen S. Nanoscale, 2013, 5: 1967â€“1971 [14] Li H, Shao F Q, Huang H, Feng J J, Wang A J. Sens. Actuators B, 2015, 226: 506â€“511 [15] Zhu A, Qu Q, Shao X, Kong B, Tian Y. Angew. Chem., 2012, 124: 7297â€“7301 [16] Luo P G, Sahu S, Yang S T, Sonkar S K, Wang J, Wang H, LeCroy G E, Cao L, Sun Y P. J. Mater. Chem. B, 2013, 1: 2116â€“2127 [17] Niu W J, Li Y, Zhu R H, Shan D, Fan Y R, Zhang X J. Sens. Actuators B, 2015, 218: 229â€“236 88.5% Â± 6.7% (n = 20). The results showed that the designed MOFF multifunctional microfluidic chip integrated with separation, magnetic capture and in situ fluorescence Fig.3 (A) Fluorescence emission spectrum of carbon quantum dots under different excitation wavelength; (B) Fluorescence micrograph of carbon quantum dots labeled HepG2; (C) Image of HepG2 connected immune-magnetic beads at 30 min; (D) Micrograph of HepG2 cells labeled by CQDs and c-met immune-magnetic beads ZHANG Ze-Jie et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2017, 45(11): 1589â€“1594 Table 1 Results of HepG2 cells captured from artificial samples on microfluidic chip Sample volume Blood cells HepG2 cells Recovery Frequency Capture rate (%) Sample 500 Î¼L 5 Ã— 106 10 10 2 88.5 Â± 6.7 9 14 8 3 7 1 Fig.4 (A) Micrographs of â€œg, hâ€� areas at sampling rate of 50 Î¼L minâ€“1; (B) Micrograph of HepG2 on microfluidic chip capture area observation was suitable for the separation, enrichment and visualization of tumor cells in the blood. 4 Conclusions A multifunctional microfluidic chip integrated with separation, magnetic capture and in situ fluorescence observation was designed and fabricated in this study. When the designed chip applied for the samples of blood cells-HepG2 suspension, a large number of blood cells could be isolated in the MOFF region at flow rate of 50 Î¼L minâ€“1, which reduced the interference in the enrichment process of HepG2 cells. HepG2 cells labeled with immune-magnetic beads were subjected to secondary enrichment by external magnetic field. The visualization of HepG2 cells was achieved by CQDs which had the advantage such as simple preparation and good biocompatibility. The capture efficiency of HepG2 cells was quantitatively analyzed by fluorescence inverted microscopy. The capture efficiency of the samples was 88.5% Â± 6.7% (n = 20). This method integrated the separation and enrichment and fluorescence observation of cells. The micro-fluidic chip can be used to analyze the sample of tumor cells-blood cells, and provides the basis for the rapid detection of CTCs. References [1] Cristofanilli M, Hayes D F, Budd G T, Ellis M J, Stopeck A, Reuben J M, Doyle G V, Matera J, Allard W J, Miller M C, Fritsche H A, Hortobagyi G N, Terstappen L W. J. Clin. Chem., 2015, 43(12): 1801â€’1807 [4] Myung J H, Hong S. Lab Chip, 2015, 15: 4500â€“4511 [5] Hu N, Yang J, Hou W S, Zheng X L, Cao Y, Yang J, Xu R, Zhang R Q. Chem. J. No. 2015AA021104), the Frontier Research Key Projects of Chongqing Science and Technology Committee (No. cstc2015jcyjBX0010), and the Scientific and Technical Innovation Projects for People's Livelihood of Chongqing Science and Technology Committee (No. cstc2015shmszx00014).

head.ce:title: Detection of HepG2 Cells in Artificial Samples by Multifunctional Microfluidic Chip

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Disciplines Lab of Novel Micro-nano Devices and System Technology, Chongqing 400044, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Disciplines Lab of Novel Micro-nano Devices and System Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International R & D center of Micro-nano Systems and New Materials Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A multifunctional microfluidic chip integrated with multi-orifice flow fractionation (MOFF) and magnetic capture technique was developed to specifically separate and capture HepG2 cells in artificial samples. The chip contained a glass substrate and a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) micro-channel cover plate. The PDMS cover plate consisted of three 10-mm-long injection channels, a MOFF separation zone and a hexagonal cavity cell enrichment detection zone. The MOFF separation zone, with a total length of 20 mm, was consisted of 80 semi-rhombic shrinkage and expansion units with a length of 0.18 mm, a depth of 50 μm, a shrinkage area width of 0.06 mm, and an expansion area of 0.20 mm. The angle between each group of shrinkage and expansion units was 103.0°. In this work, HepG2-blood cell suspension was used as the sample. Based on the principle that the magnetic beads surface modified c-Met antibody could specifically bind to HepG2 cells, an immune-magnetic beads (anti-MNCs) suspension at a concentration of 50 μg mL−1 was prepared by surface carboxylated beads, EDC (1 mg mL−1), NHS (1 mg mL−1) and c-Met antibody. At the optimized flow rate of 50 μL min−1, HepG2 in suspension samples was efficiently captured at the detection zone of chip via a magnetic field. The carbon quantum dots were prepared by microwave heating with citric acid and thiourea to label HepG2 cells which made it possible that the captured HepG2 could be visualized in-situ. The captured cells in the chip detection area were counted by microscope. It was shown that the capture rate of HepG2 cells was 88.5% ± 6.7% (500 μL volume contained 106 blood cells and 10 HepG2 cells). The results demonstrated the developed multifunctional microfluidic chip may serve as a promising tool for separation and capture of tumour cells.

A multi-functional microfluidic chip with multi-orifice flow fractionation (MOFF) and magnetic capture technique was developed. For the artificial sample of red blood cells-HepG2, under the external magnetic field, the separation-capture – in situ fluorescence detection of small amount of HepG2 cells in the sample was realized on the chip via labeling HepG2 cells with immunomagnetic beads and carbon dots.
